Cause of Death: Electrocution from energized vending machine cabinet
Notes: A 10-year-old boy (referred to as 'Brian' in EC&M article) was electrocuted at a city recreational center pool while buying candy from a vending machine. A damaged power cord from an adjacent soda machine, pinched under the candy machine leg, energized the candy machine cabinet. The broken ground pin prevented the fault from tripping a breaker. The victim was wet from swimming when he touched the machine. All parties reached a confidential settlement. Oklahoma location per ESDPA but not confirmed by EC&M article.

Two juvenile females jumped into the water off a dock at Lake Travis, Texas, and were electrocuted. Witnesses described them as having 'eyes wide open, mouth wide open, very stiff and unable to talk' before going limp and rolling over. A detective's report noted involvement of a second juvenile witness. The cause was determined to be a lack of grounding system and a faulty battery charger on a houseboat. Referenced in law enforcement training materials by Kevin Ritz (Pacific Coast Harbor Masters) and California DBW/CRLEA training documents.

Diver checking zincs in the Connecticut River at Essex, CT felt strong electric pulses in brackish water. Backed away. Pulses stopped after securing boat power.

A 23-year-old woman grabbed a dock cleat to exit the water at Egg Harbor, WI and couldn't let go (tetanic muscle contraction). Her hand went numb. A passerby kicked her hand free. No injuries. An open neutral was found as the cause.

Cause of Death: Electrocution — sailboat mast struck overhead power line
Notes: Attorney G. Brockett Irwin was electrocuted when his sailboat mast struck an overhead power line crossing Lake O' The Pines near Johnson Creek in East Texas. Three other occupants survived. Brother Tom Irwin is quoted in a 2017 article about the Boy Scout incident at the same lake. After this incident, Upshur Rural Electric Cooperative reportedly raised the power line and installed danger buoys, but clearance issues persisted, leading to the 2017 Boy Scout deaths at the same lake.

Cause of Death: Electrocution / electric shock drowning — faulted battery charger
Notes: A 44-year-old man reportedly disappeared while approaching a swim platform on the St. Croix River in Prescott, Wisconsin. A battery charger had faulted and no AC grounding wire was present. The AuGres Maritime Museum compilation suggests a date of June 8, 1986, but no independent primary sources (news, obituaries, official reports) have been found. The incident may predate digital archives.

Diver drowned at Grosse Pointe Yacht Club on Lake St. Clair, Michigan. No victim name, age, or gender identified. Information relayed third-hand per ESDPA list. Paired with a same-date diver drowning at Petoskey, MI (FI-1986-002); the two may represent a single confused report.

Diver drowned at Petoskey, Michigan, on Little Traverse Bay (Lake Michigan). No victim name, age, or gender identified. Information relayed third-hand per ESDPA list. Paired with a same-date diver drowning at Grosse Pointe Yacht Club, MI (FI-1986-001); the two may represent a single confused report.

Cause of Death: Electrocution (dove into water to rescue wife)
Notes: Detective Jack S. Deuser (41), a 20-year veteran of the Jefferson County Police Department, was electrocuted when he dove into the water at Harrods Creek Boat Harbor to rescue his wife, who was in distress. His wife also died. A faulty light switch and missing ground on a nearby houseboat energized the water. Classified as a line-of-duty death despite being off-duty, because he died attempting a rescue. Survived by three children. Buried at Cave Hill Cemetery, Louisville, KY.

An 18-year-old boy fell off a dock at Bay Haven Marina on Lake Macatawa and drowned. Two would-be rescuers experienced severe electric shock upon entering the water and were unable to complete rescue attempts. The year is uncertain (1987 or 1988).

Facility Name: State University of New York at Albany (SUNY Albany)
Notes: Bryan Higgins, 20, a sophomore from Mahopac, NY, was electrocuted while wading into Indian Lake on the SUNY Albany campus during a Tau Kappa Epsilon (TKE) fraternity initiation ritual on the evening of April 21, 1988. A faulty underground cable energized the concrete-bottom drainage pond. Two other students (Scott Payne and Vincent Mancino) entered the water to help and were shocked but survived. Higgins was pronounced dead at Albany Medical Center Hospital. Autopsy confirmed cardiac arrest due to electrocution. The university canceled Mayfest and all fraternity pledging.

Single drowning fatality in Oklahoma from a fault in a submersible pump, freshwater environment. No victim name, age, or specific location identified. The CDC MMWR documented five similar deaths at northeastern Oklahoma lakes (1989-1993) with 96% of docks violating NEC; this 1988 incident likely occurred at the same lake system.

Cause of Death: Electrocution from broken lamp wiring on dock
Body of Water: Lake B (southern Oklahoma — lake name anonymized by CDC)
Notes: A 36-year-old male was electrocuted at a lake in southern Oklahoma on June 1, 1989. He was working on a houseboat docked at the lake when his elbow contacted broken-lamp wiring connected to a 110-volt dock power line during a fuel pump installation. CDC MMWR Case 2 (Lake B, southern Oklahoma). Part of a cluster of 5 electricity-related deaths at Oklahoma lakes documented by the CDC (1989-1993).

Body of Water: Lake A (northeastern Oklahoma; likely Grand Lake O' the Cherokees)
Notes: A 32-year-old male was rewiring a submerged water pump at 'Lake A' (two adjoining lakes in northeastern Oklahoma, likely Grand Lake O' the Cherokees) and stringing electrical wire to a relay station. Co-workers found blood on rocks along the shoreline. A lake patrol diver found the victim under 25 feet of water with a laceration above his right eye. CPR was unsuccessful. Medical examiner ruled cause of death as drowning. This was an occupational incident, not recreational. A 1989 inspection of 116 commercial docks at the lake found 96% violated the NEC.

One of four boys in a canoe continued into an electric field near a dock and drowned at Lake Hamilton, Hot Springs, Arkansas. The cause was broken dock wire insulation. Lake Hamilton has been the site of at least three ESD fatalities (1991, 2001, 2008). The pattern of deaths contributed to the passage of Jesse's Law in Arkansas (2011).

Body of Water: Lake A (northeastern Oklahoma; likely Grand Lake O' the Cherokees)
Notes: A 50-year-old man was found lying unconscious on a boat dock at a northeastern Oklahoma lake, wearing wet socks and shoes. His son received an electrical shock while attempting to revive him. An electrical short was detected in the dock wiring. The medical examiner ruled the cause of death as electrocution (not drowning). The victim was found on the dock, not in the water, raising questions about the ESD classification.

Body of Water: Lake A (northeastern Oklahoma; likely Grand Lake O' the Cherokees)

Two teenage girls snorkeling near a dock at Alexandria Bay, New York (Thousand Islands region, St. Lawrence River) were paralyzed by electric shock and drowned. The dock wiring had been gnawed by rodents, creating the electrical fault. Secondary sources indicate two bystanders who entered the water to assist were also shocked and hospitalized.

Cause of Death: Electric shock drowning — dock wiring fault
Body of Water: Lake A (northeastern Oklahoma — lake name anonymized by CDC; likely Grand Lake O' the Cherokees)
Notes: A 13-year-old boy jumped from a dock into a northeastern Oklahoma lake while dock lights were illuminated. He immediately surfaced screaming, then submerged and drowned. An electrical short in the dock wiring was transmitting current into the water. This is a textbook ESD mechanism: AC current paralyzed the boy upon water entry. CDC MMWR Case 4 (Lake A, northeastern Oklahoma). Part of a cluster of 5 electricity-related deaths at Oklahoma lakes (1989-1993), where 96% of commercial docks violated the NEC.

Single drowning fatality in Texas from a fault in a boatlift, salt water environment. No victim name, age, gender, or specific location identified. ESD in salt water is scientifically unusual because salt water's high conductivity typically diverts current around rather than through a swimmer. The cause of death was listed as 'not available' in the ESDPA entry.

Body of Water: Lake A (northeastern Oklahoma; likely Grand Lake O' the Cherokees)
Notes: A 46-year-old male scuba diver was electrocuted after contacting a submersible water pump at 'Lake A' in northeastern Oklahoma. A witness reported seeing 'a blue softball-size ball of flame emitting from a pipe' containing power cables near the pump, indicating dramatic exposed and energized wiring. The medical examiner ruled the cause of death as electrocution (not drowning). This was one of five electricity-related deaths at Oklahoma lakes (1989-1993) documented by the CDC.

Facility Name: Bolling Air Force Base (now Joint Base Anacostia-Bolling)
Notes: A young boy reached from the water and grabbed the support structure of an electrical junction box at Bolling Air Force Base, Washington, DC, and was electrocuted. Bare energized wires were found touching the metal case inside the junction box, and the ground wire had been intentionally or negligently cut. The base is on the Potomac River with Capital Cove Marina. A second electrocution fatality involving a boy at the same Air Force base is documented (FI-1997-001).

Single drowning attributed to faults on a boatlift in salt water in Texas, approximately 1994. One of the very few documented ESD incidents in salt water. Could be Gulf Coast or coastal bay area given salt water designation.

Single drowning fatality at Lake Mead, Nevada, from a fault on a houseboat in a freshwater environment. No victim name or specific location within Lake Mead identified. The ESDPA date of February 1995 conflicts with earlier versions of the same list which record the identical incident as July 1997. Lake Mead is managed by the National Park Service.
Seven children behind a houseboat received electric shocks at Jamestown Resort and Marina on Lake Cumberland, Kentucky. No fatalities. A loss of neutral caused the issue. All children survived.

Facility Name: Air Force Base, Washington DC (likely Bolling AFB / Joint Base Anacostia-Bolling)
Notes: A boy walking on ice slipped and grabbed exposed wires on a dock at an Air Force Base in Washington, DC, and was electrocuted. The ESDPA lists the date as July 1997, but this is almost certainly wrong since ice conditions are impossible in Washington, DC in July. The actual month is likely January or another winter month. This is the second fatal electrocution of a boy at the same Air Force base (see also ESD-1994-00-00-1). Likely location is Bolling AFB (Capital Cove Marina area).

Cause of Death: Electric shock drowning — houseboat electrical fault
Notes: Single drowning attributed to a houseboat electrical fault in fresh water at Lake Mead, Nevada, July 1997. Despite Lake Mead being one of America's busiest recreational lakes with major marina infrastructure, this is the only documented ESD incident there. Listed as item #60 in AuGres Maritime Museum compilation and item #22 in 2CoolFishing list.
A 21-year-old was shocked on a swim ladder while exiting the water at Lake Chelan, WA. He spent 48 hours in the hospital. Rescuers were also shocked.
A 21-year-old was shocked on a swim ladder while exiting the water at Lake Chelan, WA. He spent 48 hours in the hospital. Rescuers were also shocked.

Facility Name: Lake Sonoma Marina (operated by concessionaire for US Army Corps of Engineers)
Notes: Deirdre Mary McCormick, 10, of Windsor, California, was electrocuted while swimming alongside a floating dock at Lake Sonoma Marina on a Saturday in September 1998. She touched a faulty metal conduit; a frayed wire had allowed current to flow through it. She was swimming with her sister and cousin. A rescuer felt electrical current when approaching the dock. The marina was operated by a concessionaire for the US Army Corps of Engineers. Multiple electrical defects were found. The Deirdre McCormick Memorial Play Area ('Deirdre's Park') was established near Warm Springs Dam.

Body of Water: Sacramento River / Sacramento-San Joaquin River Delta
Notes: Two boys (ages 8-10) drowned at a marina in Rio Vista, California, on the Sacramento River Delta. Before the fatal drownings, other boys had reported feeling a tingle while in the water and got out. Forty-year-old faulty wiring running under moored boats was identified as the cause, with insulation failure leading to ground faults. The wiring age is consistent with Delta Marina Yacht Harbor (built late 1950s), though the specific marina is unconfirmed.

Cause of Death: Drowning (during rescue attempt in electrified water)
Notes: A woman was in great distress in 3 feet of freshwater in South Carolina. Her husband attempted to rescue her and drowned. The ESDPA describes this as a 'single drowning' but the narrative suggests two people were affected. It is unclear whether the wife also died or survived. The original research file classified this as 2 dead, but only the husband's drowning is explicitly described in the source material. Fatality count conservatively set to 1 (husband) with wife listed as injured.

Cause of Death: Electric shock drowning — houseboat electrical fault
Notes: A young man swimming toward the stern of a houseboat at Lake Mohave became disabled and drowned. Only 17 VAC was measured behind the stern-drive, demonstrating that extremely low voltages can be lethal in fresh water. The boat had a neutral-ground bond and a homemade shore cord Y adapter that became partly disconnected, causing the hull to become energized. Listed as item #54 in AuGres Maritime Museum compilation.

A young man was disabled and drowned while swimming to the stern of a houseboat at Lake Mohave, Arizona. The cause was a homemade shore cord Y-connector that had disconnected, energizing the water. Lake Mohave is a freshwater reservoir on the Colorado River.

Two boys wearing flotation devices were found face down in the water a few feet behind a houseboat at Center Hill Lake (listed as 'Cedar Hill Lake' in ESDPA), Smithville, Tennessee. The 7-year-old could not be revived and died; the 8-year-old recovered. Electric shock drowning was suspected. The presence of flotation devices indicates shock-induced paralysis rather than inability to swim. The incident reportedly occurred at Sligo Marina (B pier).

Cause of Death: Electrocuted in water while swimming (amended from original 'drowning' ruling)
Notes: Lucas Ritz, 8, was electrocuted while swimming in the Multnomah Channel near Scappoose, Oregon, wearing a Type II life jacket. His face never entered the water. His mother Sheryl (a graduate nurse) jumped in to rescue him and was paralyzed by the current. His father Kevin performed CPR but Lucas never regained consciousness. The coroner initially ruled the death a drowning; Kevin Ritz investigated, found AC voltage in the water with a voltmeter, and the death certificate was amended to 'electrocuted in water while swimming.' A melted wire on a nearby docked boat had energized the water. Kevin Ritz subsequently co-founded the Electric Shock Drowning Prevention Association (ESDPA) and became an ABYC Certified Marine Master Technician.

Two teenage boys were swimming behind a houseboat at Whitman Hollow Marina, Norris Lake, Lafollette, Tennessee. One boy climbed onto the swim platform complaining of feeling a severe shock; the other boy fell back from the ladder — his head was reportedly not below water (possible ventricular fibrillation). He could not be resuscitated. The cause was a damaged power cable to the boat: the black lead energized the hull, the ground wire was burned in two, and the circuit breaker did not trip due to an incorrect connection. The year is uncertain (listed as 2000 in ESDPA; LOA6 LEAD-042 found ESDPA entry #6 listing April 10, 2001 as the date).

An owner was paralyzed on the bottom in 5 feet of water while rescuing a dog at the Niagara River, Grand Island, NY. A slight current moved him and he survived. Romex wiring had chafed and energized a steel piling.

Two young men (ages 21 and 22) died from electric shock drowning after jumping from their dock at Tims Ford Lake, Winchester, Tennessee. A live wire in the water near the dock was found; the cause was improperly installed electrical work on a newly constructed dock. A rescue diver also felt electric shock during recovery. Tennessee subsequently developed new residential dock installation standards.

Two young men (ages 21 and 22) died from electric shock drowning after jumping from their dock at Tims Ford Lake, Winchester, Tennessee. Tennessee subsequently developed new residential dock installation standards.

A young bystander disappeared while trying to retrieve sunglasses that went overboard near a Grand Banks 42 powerboat at Put-in-Bay, Ohio (Lake Erie). Electric shock drowning was the cause. A local tourism website references an earlier electrocution 'in front of the Crew's Nest' involving a young man diving for sunglasses and being electrocuted when he grabbed the boat, but provides no date or name. In 2017, the death of Evan Currie at Put-in-Bay was described by WTOL as 'the first electric-shock drowning ever reported in Ohio,' raising questions about whether this 2000 incident was ever officially classified as ESD.

A boy was electrocuted while using a conveyor to transfer shrimp in New Orleans, Louisiana. No ground was present on the equipment. This occurred in a salt water / commercial fishing context rather than a recreational marina setting. The ESDPA list classifies it under Electric Shock Drownings, but the description says 'electrocuted' without mentioning drowning. If the victim was a minor working in a family fishing operation, OSHA may not have been notified.

Cause of Death: Drowning (during rescue attempt in electrified water)
Notes: At Farr Shores on Lake Hamilton, Hot Springs, Arkansas, a girl was shocked after water-sliding into the water at the stern of a boat. A man who attempted to rescue her drowned. The boat had reverse polarity, a neutral-ground fault in a light fixture, and poor bonding to the service. The boat was unplugged in time to save the girl. This was the second of three ESD fatalities at Lake Hamilton (1991, 2001, 2008).

Facility Name: Private dock, Timber Ridge Drive, Montclair community
Notes: Joseph John Salute (15) and Maxwell 'Max' Scott Casillas (11) were electrocuted and drowned at approximately 3:45 PM on June 22, 2001, after jumping into Lake Montclair near a pontoon boat at a private dock. The boat's charging system was crudely rigged: an unapproved dock outlet, a spliced orange utility cord, and the cord plugged into the wrong outlet on the boat sent 117V directly into the aluminum hull. Kyle Fisher (12) also jumped in but escaped with help from a fourth boy, reporting he 'saw a red flash.' Both victims were taken to Potomac Hospital where they were pronounced dead. The lake community does not allow gasoline motors; only electric motors are permitted.

Facility Name: Bay Marine Boat Works (151 5th St, Biloxi, MS 39530)
Notes: A person was electrocuted at Bay Marine Boat Works in Biloxi, Mississippi. Reverse polarity from recent electrical work was the cause. Multiple people were reportedly shocked. The business (established 1981, owner Paul Bodin) still operates at 151 5th St, Biloxi. The date of September 15, 2001 — four days after the September 11 attacks — may explain the complete absence of media coverage.

Interviews with divers in Florida during 2002-2003 revealed many reports of high-level tingle while cleaning boat bottoms. All incidents occurred in salt water with no fatalities. This is an aggregate report, not a single incident.

A mother and her adult daughter drowned while swimming from the sundeck of a moored houseboat on Lake Cumberland during Memorial Day weekend 2002. A third daughter survived. The houseboat owner had modified the shore power connection, changing incoming power from 125V to 125/250V and miswiring the ground and hot wire, energizing the aluminum hull, railing, and ladder. Current levels in the water were estimated at 0.01-0.02A, sufficient to cause loss of muscle control in fresh water. The mother could not be saved despite CPR; the daughter was found more than 50 feet below the surface.

Cause of Death: Electrocution from faulty pool light wiring
Notes: A 14-year-old girl was electrocuted in an apartment complex swimming pool in Arlington, Texas in May 2002. Faulty wiring in underwater pool lighting energized the water. A 16-year-old male who jumped in to rescue her was severely shocked but survived after a third person used a fiberglass shepherd's hook to extract both victims. The CPSC used this case as the lead example in their nationwide pool electrocution safety campaign.

Three swimmers were in distress near a houseboat at Allatoona Lake, GA. A bystander pulled the shore cord and all three were saved. One person spent days in the hospital. Same lake as a fatal ESD incident.

A swimmer at Lake Murray, SC reported a strong tingle. Investigation found high-level fault currents in the dock frame. Fresh water environment.

Wildlife-only incident. Six ducks were killed when a shore power cord fell into the water as a houseboat pulled away from a dock at Allatoona Lake, Georgia. The 125V plug at the boat end of the shore cord had been rewired by the owner for 220V service, with L2 connected to the ground pin and the ground lead cut and taped off, causing the hull to rise to line potential. No human casualties. Ducks found floating nearby after the fact.

Two teenage boys were playing on a trampoline in a neighbor's yard when Brandenburg spotted a football in the canal. He jumped in to retrieve it and was electrocuted upon grabbing an electrified boat lift. Kopinetz called for adults and jumped in to rescue his friend but was also electrocuted when he touched the boat lift. Both were pronounced dead at Cape Coral Hospital. Lee County Sheriff's Office investigated. Rebar had penetrated through a power cable to the boat lift, energizing the lift frame in the salt water canal environment.

A marina manager at Green River Lake found a houseboat with 4 amps of leakage and 8 VAC hull potential after a child reported a tingle. The boat was rewired and leakage was cleared. Same location as another near-miss incident.

Two women swimming near a rented houseboat at Lake Barkley, Grand Rivers, KY felt strong electric shock. They swam away. A relative confirmed the same experience. Disconnecting shore power resolved the issue.

Two women swimming near a rented houseboat at Lake Barkley, Grand Rivers, KY felt strong electric shock. They swam away. A relative confirmed the same experience. Disconnecting shore power resolved the issue.

Two boys were swimming at the bow of a houseboat at Lake Wylie when they called for help. The father of one victim and a friend rushed forward. One boy on the ladder said he was being shocked. The other boy in the water was not moving. The friend rushed aft to pull the shore cord (disconnecting shore power). The father went into the water but his son could not be resuscitated. Wiring errors were apparently done by non-qualified individuals.

Cause of Death: Suspected electric shock drowning (Joule marks found on postmortem)
Notes: A young man disappeared between marina dock sections at Lake Travis, Austin, Texas. Joule marks (electrical burn marks) were found during postmortem examination, and electric shock drowning was suspected as the cause of death.

Facility Name: Private dock with motorized boat lift
Notes: A 10-year-old boy was electrocuted while swimming near a dock with a motorized boat lift at Lake Waccamaw, North Carolina. His grandfather had raised the boat lift rails about 2 feet above the water to create more swimming space. Children playing in the water reported feeling 'tingles' in their legs before the fatal incident. The grandfather looked into the water and saw the boy floating face down. CPR was unsuccessful. Investigation found the underground feeder cable's equipment ground conductor had been severed at a junction box in the house crawl space, and a water-filled junction box beneath the dock created a current path. The dock was approximately 200 feet from shore and had ceiling fans, a picnic table, and a barbecue grill. The electrical work was unpermitted and performed by an unlicensed contractor.
A diver doing dock maintenance at Saguaro Lake, AZ felt tingling near a pontoon houseboat. The shore cable was disconnected and work resumed without incident.
A man was shocked entering the water near boats on generator power on the Sacramento River. Two men jumped in to rescue him; one rescuer was also imperiled. The generator was secured. All survived.
A man was shocked entering the water near boats on generator power on the Sacramento River. Two men jumped in to rescue him; one rescuer was also imperiled. The generator was secured. All survived.

Cause of Death: Suspected electric shock drowning — Joule marks (electrical contact points) found at autopsy on right wrist, left leg, and shin
Notes: A young man in good health was swimming in the evening between two sections of a marina dock at Lake Travis, Austin, Texas. He disappeared under the water and was not recovered until two days later when his body surfaced. The post mortem found no toxic substances. However, the autopsy revealed Joule marks (electrical contact points) on the right wrist and left leg and shin — classic markers of electrical current entry/exit points on skin. The case was listed as suspected electric shock drowning under investigation. The presence of Joule marks at two distinct body locations suggests the body was the conductor between two points of electrical potential — exactly the mechanism of ESD.

Cause of Death: Electrocution from contact with energized boat ladder
Notes: Ken Lutrick, 16, was electrocuted at Main Harbor Marina at Ross Barnett Reservoir near Ridgeland, Mississippi. He was with a group of boys after church; they were fishing and then went swimming. When Ken reached for the ladder on a boat that was leaking voltage, the electrified ladder killed him. His mother, Elise Lutrick, co-founded the Electric Shock Drowning Prevention Association (ESDPA) with Kevin Ritz (father of Lucas Ritz, who died from ESD in 1999).

Tyler Deeds, 22, of Olathe, Kansas, was shocked by a dock cable and fell into the water while helping to remodel a lake house at Lake of the Ozarks, Missouri. He could not be revived. His death directly prompted 2006 code changes at Lake of the Ozarks, though subsequent inspections found approximately 75% of the roughly 25,000 docks did not comply with safety guidelines.
A man was badly shocked and paralyzed on a boat ladder at Lake Ontario, NY. Power was turned off and he regained feeling. No ground wire was found in the dock wiring.

Samantha 'Sam' Chipley, 19, of Wilmore, Kentucky, was electrocuted while swimming near a houseboat at Scotts Creek Marina on Cave Run Lake. On June 27, 2005, Chipley and three friends (Margaret, Susie, Courtney) arrived at the marina to spend the night on a houseboat owned by Susie's father. The four jumped into the water despite 'No Swimming' signs. Chipley began jerking in the water. Margaret jumped off a raft to help and felt a shock through her entire body. Another boater dove in to save Chipley, was shocked, and barely escaped. The houseboat's faulty battery charger was energizing the hull. Chipley was pronounced dead June 28, 2005.

A diver in Brooklyn, NY complained of tingling and pain. 40 VAC was measured in the water. A neutral-ground short on a recently installed water heater was identified as the cause.

Facility Name: Private dock with electric boat lift and lighting
Notes: 16-year-old DeSoto HS sophomore died swimming at a private dock in Spring Lake, Jefferson County MO. Three friends were with him; two were rendered unconscious by electrical current and airlifted to St. John's Mercy Medical Center. A fourth teen was present but not seriously injured. Investigation found 10 amps of current in the dock's supply cable, 4-6 VAC measured 2 feet from the swim ladder, and a chain leading into the water with arcing evidence. AmerenUE's 7,200V underground distribution cable had a defective/missing neutral, causing return current to flow through earth and concentrate at the bonded dock ladder.

Cause of Death: Electrocution (per Cherokee County Coroner Don Rogers)
Notes: Jeremy David Whiteside, 24, of Rome, Georgia, was killed on Weiss Lake in Cherokee County, Alabama. He and friends had been wake boarding/skiing on the lake and were returning to shore around 8 p.m. on Sunday, May 21, 2006. Friend Mitch Bowles (of Rome) was swimming to the pier and felt a shock while reaching for the ladder. Thomas Ayer (of Rome) and Whiteside plunged in to rescue Bowles but were shocked within 5 feet of the pier. When the electricity stopped, others pulled Bowles onto the dock. Bowles and another man dragged Whiteside out and administered CPR. Whiteside was pronounced dead at Cherokee Medical Center at 8:40 p.m. Cherokee County Coroner Don Rogers ruled the cause of death as electrocution.

A 56-year-old man was killed while swimming from the stern of a boat at Racine Harbor on Lake Michigan. An inquest ruled the death as accidental electrocution. A reverse polarity light reportedly flickered on and went out when power was applied to the vessel.

Cause of Death: Electrocution/drowning (officials could not definitively determine which)
Notes: Jesse Ryan Fortner, 14, of Saline County, Arkansas, died at approximately 12:30 p.m. on Saturday, June 25, 2006, at Brady Mountain Resort on Lake Ouachita in Royal, Arkansas. Fortner and a friend were swimming near a houseboat dock when both came in contact with electric current from an ungrounded electrical cord. The electricity reached a ladder leading into the water. Fortner's friend was hospitalized at St. Joseph's Medical Center but was expected to be released same day. An unnamed bystander attempting rescue was knocked unconscious. Officials could not definitively determine whether Fortner drowned or died from electrocution. Garland County Sheriff Judy Daniell investigated.

Three men in a boat at Lake L'Homme Dieu, Douglas County, MN had their propeller catch an extension cord powering a boat lift. They were severely shocked upon entering the water. One was hospitalized for several days (described as face down and unresponsive). All survived.

Cause of Death: Electrocution from faulty shore power cord at marina dock
Facility Name: River Street Marina, near 10th Street Bridge
Notes: Michael John McNash, 20, of Port Huron Township, MI, died attempting to board a docked boat at River Street Marina near the 10th Street Bridge on the Black River. A faulty shore power cord energized the boat and surrounding water. Despite the main power switch being turned off, electricity continued flowing through the defective cord, and the circuit breaker failed to trip. A Michigan Department of Labor and Economic Growth electrical expert inspected the scene. Police found no criminal wrongdoing. McNash was a 2004 Port Huron Northern High School graduate, multi-sport athlete, and three-summer lifeguard at Lakeside Beach.

Cause of Death: Electrocution/drowning from energized swim ladder at private dock
Notes: Heather Huff, 24, of Raymore, Missouri, was electrocuted at the 81 Mile Marker of Lake of the Ozarks. She was paralyzed by electric shock when she touched a metal swim ladder on a private dock that was energized. Her father was present and family members pulled her from the water and began CPR immediately, but she was pronounced dead at 5:02 p.m.

Cause of Death: Electrocution from exposed wire while working on personal watercraft near metal dock
Notes: Tyler James Howarth, 15, of Gainesville, GA, was electrocuted at his family's lake home on Lake Lanier near Cumming, Forsyth County, Georgia. He was standing in the water working on a personal watercraft beside a metal dock when he apparently grabbed an exposed wire. He fell back and under the dock. Two 15-year-old friends on the dock tried to pull him out but were also shocked. His father arrived, jumped in and pulled his son from the lake and began CPR, but Howarth was pronounced dead at Northeast Georgia Medical Center. He was a student at North Forsyth High School and member of the Raiders Football Team. The Tyler Howarth Community Fund raised over $27,000 for community causes.

An owner checking underwater with scuba gear at Racine, WI on Lake Michigan touched a bronze propeller and was nearly paralyzed. The cause was a boat in the next slip with a faulty water heater neutral wire. Same location as a fatal incident (ESD #75, May 2006).

A SCUBA diver felt tingling then moderate shock while approaching a moored sailboat at Collins Bay on Lake Ontario, Kingston, Ontario, Canada. The diver backed away. A battery charger had a short circuit.

An adult male was immediately paralyzed at the end of a dock at Lake Arcadia, Edmond, OK. His wife kept his head above water from the dock. A bystander was also shocked. High water had submerged electrical outlets.

An adult male was immediately paralyzed at the end of a dock at Lake Arcadia, Edmond, OK. His wife kept his head above water from the dock. A bystander was also shocked. High water had submerged electrical outlets.

Cause of Death: Electric shock drowning — paralyzed by electrical shock at metal ladder, fell back into water and drowned
Notes: A 24-year-old woman attempted to exit the water using a metal ladder at the end of a private dock on Lake of the Ozarks, Missouri. She experienced a paralyzing electric shock which caused her to fall back into the water and drown. Several people had previously reported being shocked by the same ladder, and the dock owner had gone to shut the power off (but apparently did not do so in time). Investigation found the dock power wiring termination was submerged under the dock near the exit point. This is the fourth documented ESD fatality at Lake of the Ozarks, following a ~2004 incident and preceding the 2012 Anderson children deaths and 2015 Colburn death.

Ryan O'Gorman, 22, a recent Cornell University graduate, entered a private farm pond in Eden, NY to rescue a dog. He was thrown back and suffered cardiac arrest (no heartbeat for 20 minutes). His father, Dr. O'Gorman (a physician), performed CPR along with Lt. John McCarthy (Eden police officer/volunteer firefighter). The dog was killed by the electrical shock. A submersible irrigation pump with deteriorated wiring (installed before 1994) was the cause. Ryan walked out of Mercy Hospital four days later.

A boat owner cleaning a prop shaft in the water at Franklin Lock Campground on the Caloosahatchee River, FL was shocked. His teeth clenched involuntarily and he saw blue sparks. He exited the water. The breaker had tripped.

Cause of Death: Electrocution — electric drill on floating dock without GFCI
Facility Name: Ocean Isle Beach Marina, 2000 Sommerset Rd SW
Notes: A construction worker employed by Coastal Carolina Construction & Development, Inc. was electrocuted while performing repair work on a floating dock at Ocean Isle Beach Marina. He was using an electric drill with his feet in the water, plugged into a marina receptacle that lacked GFCI protection. He was pronounced dead at Brunswick Community Hospital. OSHA issued three serious violations totaling $3,360 in penalties, including citation for 29 CFR 1926.404(B)(1)(ii) — GFCI requirement for electrical equipment in wet locations.

Sara Crotts, 14, of Sherwood, Arkansas, died from electrocution with drowning at Lake Hamilton Resort on Saturday, August 23, 2008. She was swimming with Tamara Keele, 40, and her son Devin Keele, 6, both of Sherwood, at approximately 8 p.m. The 7-year-old boy (per ESDPA, 6 per news) was being shocked and Crotts attempted to help him. 103 VAC was measured on the ladder. An unbonded metal pole welded to the dock with a halogen light was involved in the electrical fault.

Facility Name: Neighbor's dock at 3830 N. Shore Drive
Notes: Nickolas Greiner, 30, from Ankeny, jumped into Clear Lake at his parents' home (3830 N. Shore Drive) to rescue a dog struggling underwater near a neighbor's dock hoist. A family member recognized the electrical hazard and disconnected the dock power. Greiner was treated at Mercy Medical Center-North Iowa in Mason City with burns and bruising. The dog died.

Cause of Death: Electrocution (autopsy showed no water in lungs)
Notes: Michael Andrew Cunningham, 15, of Bridgeport, WV, a sophomore at Bridgeport High School, was electrocuted at Stonewall Jackson Lake Marina on Saturday, May 29, 2010. He was swimming with three other youths behind a docked boat. Cunningham had exited the water and was standing on the back of the boat when he touched the swim ladder and received an electric shock, falling into the water and disappearing. Body recovered at 34-foot depth nearly 5 hours later. Autopsy showed no water in his lungs — death was from electrocution, not drowning. Current from the dock had leaked into the water around the houseboat. Another youth was also shocked but survived. His mother Amy Cunningham became a major ESD safety advocate.

Facility Name: Private covered dock with boat lift, approximately 200 feet from shore
Notes: A boy was killed swimming near a covered dock with a boat lift at Lake Waccamaw, North Carolina, on May 29, 2010. A grandfather had taken children to play under the dock in 3 feet of water. He raised the metal boat lift rails about 2 feet above water to create swimming space. One child reported feeling a 'tingle' in his legs before the fatal incident. The grandfather saw a child floating face down and another child raised the boy's head, finding him unconscious. CPR was unsuccessful. Investigation found the underground feeder cable's equipment ground conductor had been severed at a junction box in the house crawl space, and a water-filled junction box beneath the dock created a current path. The dock was approximately 200 feet from shore with amenities including ceiling fans, picnic table, and barbecue grill. The installation was unpermitted, done by an unlicensed contractor, and lacked required GFCI protection.

Cause of Death: Suspected electrocution (fell from dock ladder into water)
Notes: A 16-year-old boy fell back into the water while climbing a dock ladder at Lewis Smith Lake, Alabama, in June 2010. Electrocution was suspected as the cause of death. No further details are available.
A 54-year-old man died jumping into a swimming pool in York, South Carolina, to rescue his granddaughter who was being shocked by the pool's railing. The granddaughter survived the incident.
A 54-year-old man died jumping into a swimming pool in York, South Carolina, to rescue his granddaughter who was being shocked by the pool's railing. The granddaughter survived the incident.

A 15-year-old girl and her 8-year-old sister were shocked near a boat dock at Lake Travis, TX. The older sister was unresponsive and in critical condition in the hospital. Shoddy boat lift wiring was identified as the cause. Both girls survived.

Three females (ages 12, 20, and 30) were shocked near a docked boat at International Harbor, Friendsville, TN. One was not breathing when EMS arrived and was revived. A 63-year-old woman was also shocked. All four victims were hospitalized but all survived.

Beth Waite was swimming behind a houseboat at Green River Lake when she was shocked, arched back, and began sinking. Her boyfriend John Childress felt shocks from the boat ramp but avoided entering the water. Houseboat owner Bobby Gullett turned off the power and jumped in to rescue Beth, but was also severely shocked by voltage still present in the water. Both Beth and Bobby received emergency medical care. All survived.

Cause of Death: Electrocution caused by faulty electric wiring — shocked then drowned
Notes: Zachary Joshua 'Zach' Crays, 13, of Lafayette IN, was electrocuted while swimming with friends near a dock at Angel Shores Resort on Lake Freeman. A water-soaked junction box energized the dock metal. He felt a tingle, tried to exit, and fell back into the water. He died at 10:55 AM at White County Memorial Hospital. Classic electric shock drowning case.
A father and two 13-year-old sons were shocked at Lake Champlain, Vermont. All three survived. No further details available about electrical source, specific location, or circumstances.

Body of Water: Small lake at Perrine Wayside Dog Park
Facility Name: Perrine Wayside Dog Park (Village Dog Park), 16425 S. Dixie Highway
Notes: A dog (Ruger, 6-month-old Labrador retriever belonging to Victor Garcia) died in the lake at Perrine Wayside Dog Park, Palmetto Bay FL. A man was stopped by shocks when attempting to enter the water. The fountain was still electrifying the water after being turned off. The fountain, pump, and wiring were completely removed and not replaced. Park was closed for most of September 2010.

A 12-year-old girl grabbed an electrical wire at Hartwell Lake, Anderson, SC and went into cardiac arrest. She was resuscitated by her father and airlifted to the hospital. A grounding problem on the dock was found. She survived.
A dog died in a retention pond fountain in Houston, TX. A man felt strong current at ankle depth and retreated. Power was secured.

Cause of Death: Electrocution while attempting to rescue dog from electrified water near boat lift
Notes: Brian Eugene Harwick, 50, of Lake City, Minnesota, was electrocuted on Saturday, May 28, 2011, at a family home at 1400 Central Point on the north side of Lake City. His dog ran into the water and swam over a boat lift that had an electrical track running underwater to the shore. The dog received a shock and began to sink. Another person jumped in first to rescue the dog but was also shocked and managed to exit the water. Harwick then jumped in, was shocked and rendered unconscious, and went underwater. Friends and relatives pulled him from the water and attempted CPR, but he died at Mayo Clinic Health System's hospital. Harwick worked at the Kemps ice cream plant. His brother Tom had died in February and his mother in March of the same year.

A woman was overcome by electrical current in the water via a boat ladder at Lou Wendell Marine on the Kanawha River, Charleston, WV. She was submerged for 90 seconds before being rescued. A grounded neutral in the pedestal was identified as the cause.

Cause of Death: Electrocution while attempting to rescue friend
Facility Name: Cornfield with center pivot irrigation system
Notes: Hannah Kendall and Jade Garza, both 14, of Sterling, Illinois, were electrocuted on Monday, July 25, 2011, while detasseling corn in a field near Tampico. The girls were best friends working with a crew of approximately 70 others. They stepped into a pool of standing water in the field that had been electrified by a defective meter on the center pivot irrigation system. Lightning had reportedly struck the meter box, energizing the system. Jade received the initial shock, and Hannah was killed trying to save her. Eight other workers were injured, one critically. OSHA investigated but issued no citations. Both funerals, held July 29, opened with the same song: 'If I Die Young.' The girls would have been freshmen at Sterling High School that fall.

Cause of Death: Electrocution from contact with energized equipment on dock
Notes: A 25-year-old woman was electrocuted at Lake Sinclair, Putnam County, Georgia, on August 15, 2011. She reached from the water and touched either a box or bare wire between two docked houseboats. The dock was being worked on at the time. An 8-year-old girl swimming in the area received a shock but survived. A second woman was shocked when she pulled the 25-year-old away from the dock and attempted to hold her head above water.

Cause of Death: Combination of electrical shock and drowning
Notes: Michael Scott Knudsen, 18, of Mancelona, MI, was electrocuted at the Duncan L. Clinch Marina on August 15, 2011. Knudsen and friend Zachary Kott-Millard jumped from F-Dock into West Grand Traverse Bay at approximately 7:30 p.m. Knudsen immediately struggled, bleeding from his eyes, ears, and nose due to electrical shock. Friends who tried to grab his arms were also shocked. Marina employees cut power, and two firefighters found Knudsen unresponsive beneath the dock. Autopsy determined death from 'a combination of electrical shock and drowning.' Investigation found a short circuit in a 220-volt line with corroded insulation in metal junction boxes (the design specified fiberglass), a failed circuit breaker, and corroded equipment ground materials. Swimming was prohibited at the marina. Prior users had reported feeling electrical current near the dock.

During Hurricane Irene, a dog was electrocuted in floodwater near Shady Beach, Norwalk, CT. Two women felt shocks. Witnesses reported the 'water was boiling' from electrical current. Hurricane Irene caused widespread flooding and 767,000 power outages across Connecticut.

Three women were shocked while swimming near a dock at Lake of the Ozarks, MO on Memorial Day weekend. Someone turned off the power. One woman needed CPR. All survived. No GFCI was present and the ground wire was inadequate.

Facility Name: Orange Lake Resort, miniature golf course
Notes: 11-year-old Ashton Jojo from Latham, NY, was electrocuted while playing miniature golf at Orange Lake Resort. She stepped into a 2-foot-deep pond to retrieve her golf ball. A bystander, Christopher Burges, attempted rescue but was also shocked. Cause was a faulty pump connected to the wrong circuit breaker with missing GFCI.

Facility Name: Private dock, Gravois Arm, 6.5 Mile Marker
Notes: Alexandra Anderson (13) and her brother Brayden Anderson (8), both of Ashland MO, were electrocuted while swimming near the family's vacation home dock at the 6.5 mile marker of the Gravois Arm, Lake of the Ozarks. A chafed hot wire had shorted to the unbonded dock framing, sending stray current into the water. Several adults pulled the children out and performed CPR. Both were pronounced dead at Lake Regional Hospital, Osage Beach. Mother Angela Anderson became a leading national ESD safety advocate and testified before Missouri lawmakers for dock safety legislation (Rep. Jake Hummel bill).

Two boys, friends and classmates at Cornerstone Academy in Morristown TN, died after being electrocuted while swimming at German Creek Marina on Cherokee Lake. Noah died July 4; Nate died July 5 on life support. Nate's 8-year-old sister was also shocked and hospitalized overnight. Frayed wiring on a houseboat combined with lack of GFI protection at the marina. Incident led to Tennessee's Noah Dean and Nate Act (2014).

Facility Name: Private dock, Dry Branch Cove, 5-mile marker
Notes: Jennifer Lankford, 26, of Hazelwood, Missouri, was electrocuted on Saturday, July 7, 2012, at approximately 7:45 p.m. at Dry Branch Cove, Morgan County, at the 5-mile marker of Lake of the Ozarks. Lankford was swimming with two children (her stepbrothers, ages 11 and 13) when she touched a dock with faulty wiring and was electrocuted. The children survived. Missouri Highway Patrol Sgt. Paul Reinsch investigated. This was the second fatal electrocution at Lake of the Ozarks that week — three days earlier, on July 4, siblings Alexandra Anderson (13) and Brayden Anderson (8) had been killed at a different dock.

Two children and an adult near a dock under electrical repairs at Lake of the Ozarks, MO all felt electric shock. Power was turned off and everyone exited safely. Occurred 19 days after fatal Anderson children incident (July 4) and 16 days after Jennifer Lankford fatality (July 7).

Cause of Death: Electrocution from extension cord in water near boat lift
Notes: Robert Stoen, 22, of Owatonna, MN, was electrocuted when an extension cord fell into the water from a boat lift at a private dock on Pokegama Lake (Hill Top Trail, Cohasset, MN). Eric Hancock (35) and Cheryl Hancock (57), also of Owatonna, were shocked and hospitalized in serious but stable condition. The Itasca County Sheriff's Office responded. This occurred less than a month after the July 4, 2012 Cherokee Lake (TN) ESD deaths and was part of a 2012 summer cluster of ESD fatalities.

A woman and her husband both confirmed shock while reaching for a dock ladder at Gravois Arm on Lake of the Ozarks, MO. They swam away to a neighbor's dock and survived. Same area as July 4, 2012 fatal incident (Anderson children).

Bianca Cruz (15) and her sister Tiffany Cruz (23) were swimming in their backyard pool in Garden Grove, CA around noon when electrical current ran through the water. Bianca was at the deep end and became unresponsive, requiring CPR by Garden Grove police officers Claudia Alarcon and Edgar Valencia. Their father Julio Cruz was shocked pulling Bianca from the pool. Both sisters were hospitalized at UCI Medical Center. Bianca was expected to make a full recovery.

Cause of Death: Electrocution from contact with electrified water from downed power lines
Facility Name: Magnolia Boulevard at Ben Avenue, Valley Village
Notes: Two Good Samaritan women were electrocuted at approximately 8:25 p.m. on Wednesday, August 22, 2012, in Valley Village (Los Angeles). Driver Arman Samsonian lost control of his SUV at high speed, crashed into a fire hydrant and light pole at the intersection of Magnolia Boulevard and Ben Avenue. Water from the fire hydrant contacted exposed electrical lines, creating an electrified puddle estimated at 4,800 volts. Irma Yolanda Zamora, 40, of Burbank, and Stacey Lee Schreiber, 39, of Valley Village, witnessed the crash and rushed to help the driver. They were electrocuted by the energized water. Approximately eight other people, including an LAPD officer, were also shocked while attempting to help. LAFD firefighters received medals of valor for their rescue efforts.

Facility Name: Irrigation canal approximately 3 miles NW of Blackfoot
Notes: Three people were electrocuted in an irrigation canal approximately 3 miles northwest of Blackfoot, Idaho, on Thursday, August 30, 2012, at approximately 9:30 p.m. Jacquelyn R. Paulson, 31, an Idaho State University assistant track and field coach and two-time NCAA All-American in the heptathlon, went missing while searching for the family dog. The dog had entered the electrified water and was shocked. Michael Lance Hicks, 41, and Preston Keith Tarpley, 49, both of Blackfoot, entered the water to rescue Paulson and were also electrocuted. The cause was a broken ground wire between the transformer pole and the service pole, which energized the ground wire between the service pole and the irrigation pump, electrifying the canal. Circuit breakers did not trip.

Cause of Death: Electrocution/drowning from faulty dock wiring caused by lightning damage
Notes: William 'Will' Nolan, 39, a professor of film, literary, and cultural studies in the Honors College at the University of Alabama, was electrocuted at Tims Ford Lake on September 27, 2012. He was visiting family in Winchester, Tennessee, and went for a swim around 7 p.m. after returning from a jog. After about an hour, his mother-in-law walked to the dock and found his clothing. Authorities found him under the dock. Investigation revealed the house had been struck by lightning in the recent past, causing faulty wiring from the house to the dock that energized the area with electricity. Born May 27, 1973, in West Chelmsford, Massachusetts. He was an Eagle Scout and left behind a wife and newborn son (born August 2012).

Facility Name: South Georgia Technical College, decorative fountain
Notes: Adriana Rhine, 18, a student at South Georgia Technical College, was electrocuted on September 27, 2012, after entering a decorative fountain on campus to retrieve her young son's ball. When bystanders attempted to help her, they also received electrical shocks, preventing immediate rescue. She was transported to Phoebe Sumter Medical Center where she was pronounced dead. Post-incident inspection found 17 electrical code violations that made the fountain a hazard.

Kyle Richard McGonigle, 36, of Vine Grove, Kentucky, was electrocuted on Monday night, May 20, 2013, at Rough River Lake near Peter Cave in Grayson County. McGonigle was swimming and fishing with three co-workers when he heard his friend's dog yelping in the water about five feet from a boat. He dove in to rescue the dog and both he and the dog drowned. Autopsy confirmed death from low-voltage electrocution. Two energized shore power cords had fallen into the water — one from a dock power pedestal, the second from a houseboat. Electrical inspection found multiple violations: spliced cords, non-functioning GFCI receptacles, shore power receptacles in poor condition, and broken covers. Alcohol was found at the scene.

On Memorial Day (May 27, 2013), Gary Muter and his 6-year-old granddaughter Ava Muter were swimming approximately 10 feet from Evelyn Morrison's dock at Lake Lanier, Gainesville, GA. Gary felt a jolt in his elbow, recognized it as electric shock, grabbed Ava and got both out of the water. Morrison's electrician ruled out her dock as the source (it had GFCI protection). The actual source of the electrical current was never identified. A power outage occurred simultaneously on the street.

On June 18, 2013, a woman in a flooded basement in Scott City, Missouri was shocked while unplugging cords. She rescued her cat and survived by floating on furniture. Non-marina context (flooded basement). No flooding event documented in Scott City for June 2013; date or location may be inaccurate.

Kevin Curtis Short, 34, of Lily, KY, was electrocuted at a private dock on Lake Cumberland. A frayed extension cord energized the aluminum dock ladder. In his final moments, Short pushed his young son past the ladder to safety. He was a National Guard veteran and Walmart Distribution Center manager. His death was cited in the Kentucky legislature as motivation for House Bill 107 (Samantha Chipley Act).

Man felt electric shock and paralysis while swimming off a docked boat at Lake Norman, NC in July 2013. He managed to move far enough away from the electrical source to regain control of his muscles. Classic ESD near-miss with paralysis followed by escape. Date is approximate (month only).

Cause of Death: Electrocution — died July 7, 2013 at Hennepin County Medical Center
Notes: Daniel Petersen, 46, of Big Lake, MN, died July 7 after being electrocuted July 4, 2013, while attempting to charge his houseboat battery at Eagle Lake. He slipped while standing in 20 inches of water and immersed a plugged-in battery charger. His 49-year-old sister was also shocked attempting rescue but survived.

Daniel Petersen, 46, of Big Lake, MN, died July 7 after being electrocuted July 4, 2013, while attempting to charge his houseboat battery at Eagle Lake. On July 11, 2013, eight college girls were swimming at the 35 mile marker on Lake of the Ozarks, MO. One girl's arm went numb. Others were shocked while exiting the water at a nearby dock. All survived without serious injury.

Cause of Death: Electrocution — ground current from downed power line energized metal ladder
Notes: Lauren Suzanne Cecil, 11, was electrocuted at Brookside Swim Club when a city-owned power line connector failed, causing the line to fall and energize the pool's metal ladder through saturated ground. Three girls were in the pool; two escaped via the concrete side, but Lauren grabbed the metal ladder. Not a classic dock/marina ESD — overhead power line failure affecting a pool.

Paige Scheel, 12, was electrocuted while filling an inflatable pool in her backyard in Syracuse, NY. She picked up an extension cord or electric pump plugged into an outlet and immediately began shaking, then fell face-first into the pool. A friend tried to pull her out but received a shock. She was pronounced dead at Upstate University Hospital.

Cause of Death: Electrocution from downed power line via wet ground
Notes: Stephanie Diaz-Ruiz, 19, was babysitting when a severe storm knocked down power lines. She stepped outside to investigate a power outage and was electrocuted by current traveling through wet ground 25 feet from the downed line. First responders could not reach her until Georgia Power cut the power. Not a classic ESD case — downed power line on land, not dock/marina.

Cause of Death: Electrocution — died Sept 6, 2013 after removal from life support
Notes: Raul Hernandez Martinez, 27, died after rescuing his 11-year-old brother David Duran from the electrified pool at Hilton Houston Westchase on Labor Day weekend. Pool lights turned on around 7:40 PM, surging current through the water. Martinez fought through the current to push David to safety but was overcome. He was removed from life support September 6. Mother Maria Isabel Duran was knocked unconscious attempting rescue. Criminal charges filed against two electricians from Brown Electrical Inc.

7-year-old Calder Sloan was electrocuted in his family's pool at their North Miami home. He had celebrated his 7th birthday one week earlier. The pool light switch was not grounded and the grounding cable was loose. No permits had been pulled in the prior 12 months. A man performing CPR was also shocked when he put his hand in the pool. The Caleb & Calder's Awesome Foundation was established in his memory.

Facility Name: Palms West Condominiums pool, 1950 W. 56th St.
Notes: On April 27, 2014, three children were shocked at the Palms West Condominiums pool in Hialeah, FL. Diego Cabrera (10), Daniella Gamez (6), and a 5-year-old girl were shocked by an improperly grounded pool pump. The 5-year-old touched the metal hand rail and was paralyzed by electricity. An adult man (believed to be her grandfather) attempted rescue and was also shocked but managed to pull her out. All three children were hospitalized at Miami Children's Hospital for four nights. Surveillance video captured the incident. Hialeah city inspectors blamed unpermitted and shoddy electrical work on pool equipment.

CCTV footage captured children suffering electric shocks after touching a metal handrail in a Florida apartment swimming pool. One girl's body froze upon contact; her father pulled her free while getting shocked. A second girl remained paralyzed on the railing until her grandfather dragged her out and collapsed from the current. A 10-year-old boy was also shocked. All three children survived. Police blamed unconnected ground wires in the pool's pump house. The footage became one of the most-viewed ESD-related videos and is frequently used in awareness campaigns.

CCTV footage captured children suffering electric shocks after touching a metal handrail in a Florida apartment swimming pool. One girl's body froze upon contact; her father pulled her free while getting shocked. A second girl remained paralyzed on the railing until her grandfather dragged her out and collapsed from the current. A 10-year-old boy was also shocked. All three children survived. Police blamed unconnected ground wires in the pool's pump house. The footage became one of the most-viewed ESD-related videos and is frequently used in awareness campaigns.

Facility Name: Bullfrog Marina, Glen Canyon National Recreation Area
Notes: Alec McQueen, 22, of Salt Lake City, was electrocuted at Bullfrog Marina on Lake Powell. He jumped into the water from his boat's swim deck and was immediately overcome. A friend and two rescuers were also shocked. A boat in the adjacent slip had a 30-amp cord improperly inserted into a worn 50-amp outlet. McQueen's body was recovered two days later, 113 feet below the surface.

On July 4, 2014, several swimmers in a cove near the 7 Mile Marker at Lake of the Ozarks felt electric shock in the water. Turning off dock power didn't resolve the issue. The source was a live wire shorted to a ramp approximately 100 yards away, sending 120V AC into the water. The dock at the ramp had been disconnected years prior, but the electrical line was never properly disconnected from the main breaker. The breaker had been turned off and the wire wrapped around the ramp frame; someone later turned it back on, energizing the water for an estimated month before discovery. All swimmers recognized the danger and exited safely.

Cause of Death: Electrocution (confirmed by Harris County ME, Sept 17, 2014)
Notes: Andrew Orvis, 8, was electrocuted at Piney Shores Resort on Lake Conroe. He sat on the dock and dangled his feet in the water, then fell in with unnatural body motion. In the water approximately 15 seconds before his father pulled him out and performed CPR. He was airlifted to Memorial Hermann Hospital where he died. Harris County ME confirmed electrocution Sept 17, 2014; drowning was NOT a contributing cause.

Madeline 'Maddy' Roskie, 18, died while inner-tubing on the Puyallup River. She complained of leg numbness then lost consciousness. Her boyfriend also felt tingling. Death certificate listed cause as 'undetermined,' though a PSE technician later found 280V from an ungrounded irrigation pump on adjacent property. Father Earl Roskie filed lawsuit against Puget Sound Energy.

Cause of Death: Electrocution — boat lift malfunction energized pontoon boat ladder
Notes: Sarah Grace Tellifero, 13, daughter of Pastor Jon Tellifero, was electrocuted at Lake Bruin when a boat lift malfunction energized a pontoon boat's ladder. A 17-year-old was also shocked but survived. Other swimmers had previously reported tingling in the water — an unrecognized warning sign.

Dianne Guyton was swimming in Smith Lake, Alabama, approximately 2015, when she reached up to the dock to get her phone and was electrocuted by electricity in the water. Her neighbors brought her to shore. She survived but nearly drowned. As of June 2024, she was publicly sharing her story to warn others about ESD risks. Smith Lake is the same lake where Carmen Johnson died in April 2016 (ESD-2016-04-16-1) — Guyton's incident may have preceded Johnson's death by approximately one year.

Facility Name: Rental property dock, Woods Hollow Cove, 22.2 Mile Marker
Notes: Marcus Colburn, 21, of East Moline, IL, died on Father's Day while swimming at a rental property dock on Lake of the Ozarks. Both Colburn and Taylor Curley felt electricity in the water. When Colburn grabbed the dock ladder, he was shocked and fell back in face-down. Someone shut off power, saving Curley. Circuit breaker had been tripping repeatedly — a warning sign that went unaddressed.

Dr. Eric Jonathan Hughes, 37, a Tuscaloosa orthodontist, was found in approximately 4 feet of water near his pontoon boat in Lake Tuscaloosa after going swimming to cool off from cutting grass. Investigators determined electric shock contributed to drowning. Lake Tuscaloosa had multiple ESD incidents, including two more deaths in 2017.

On August 25, 2015, Joel St. Jean, a commercial diver in his mid-20s from Portsmouth, NH, was shocked while cleaning the bottom of a 36-foot Catalina sailboat at Portsmouth Naval Shipyard marina (physically located in Kittery, Maine). The sailboat was connected to shore power. St. Jean was found floating unconscious with his scuba regulator still in his mouth. He suffered prolonged headaches, memory loss, and fatigue. He filed a $500,000 lawsuit against the U.S. government alleging negligent maintenance of the marina's electrical supply.

Facility Name: Private residence, 2300 block of Yosemite Drive
Notes: Jim Tramel, 43, VP of Sales at RevJet from Burlingame CA, died on Easter Sunday after jumping into an electrified pool in Palm Springs to save his daughter. Seven people total were shocked at the family gathering. The home was built in 1963 with outdated pool electrical systems. His daughter survived but was in critical condition.

Carmen Elizabeth Johnson, 15, a Priceville High School cheerleader, was electrocuted at her family's lakehouse dock on Smith Lake. She and friend Reagan Gargis jumped off the top deck into electrically charged water. Her father Jimmy and brother Zach also jumped in and felt 'piercing electric shocks.' Jimmy yelled to his wife to cut power. Carmen died April 17. The Johnson family became ESD awareness advocates.

Cause of Death: Electrocution from short in light fixture near pool
Facility Name: Aztec Motel, 400 block of East Lavender Road
Notes: Greg Subiszak, 34, of Fair Lawn NJ, was found unconscious at the bottom of the Aztec Motel pool on Memorial Day weekend. Teenager Ivan Martinez jumped in to save him but went into convulsions from electrical shock. A maintenance worker eventually cut the electricity. Subiszak later died from his injuries. Police determined no criminal charges were warranted.

Cause of Death: Electrocution from faulty pool light circuit (outcome uncertain — last known critical)
Notes: An 8-year-old girl was among 8 children swimming when a child flipped the pool light switch, sending current into the water. She was the only one unable to get out. Family began CPR; police used AED; she was airlifted to Penn State Hershey Medical Center in critical condition. Seven other children were treated on scene. Final outcome not confirmed in available sources — classified as fatal per ESDPA listing but may have survived.

Two adults drowned at a private dock on Chickamauga Lake, Sale Creek, Hamilton County TN. Randy Freeney (62) entered the water and was incapacitated; Donna Berger (34), his neighbor, jumped in to rescue him and also drowned. Freeney's wife also attempted to help but was pulled back. Son made 911 call. Classic ESD pattern: two victims simultaneously incapacitated in same water near powered dock, with skilled swimmer (Berger) unable to function after entering water. No electrical investigation was documented in any public record. WSOC-TV Charlotte used this incident in a 9 Investigates ESD documentary segment.

Facility Name: Private residence, near 43rd Avenue and Thomas Road
Notes: A 27-year-old father was electrocuted while attempting to change a pool light. His wife and three children witnessed the incident and were also shocked when trying to rescue him. His 8-year-old daughter reported he sank in the deep end. Official cause classified as drowning with electrocution as contributing factor. Victim name not released.

Cause of Death: Electric shock drowning — contacted energized boat lift, fell into water
Notes: A 24-year-old construction worker employed by LHC Construction Management, LLC, was electrocuted at approximately 4:00 PM while installing tongue-and-groove wood trim on the ceiling of a new dock area under construction. He came into contact with an energized boat lift, received an electric shock, fell into the water, and drowned. This is mechanistically identical to ESD: electrical current from dock infrastructure incapacitates a person who then drowns.

Cause of Death: Electrocution complicated by terminal immersion in water
Facility Name: Heritage Point community pool, Valley Forge Road
Notes: Rachel Rosoff, 17, an Enloe High School senior working as a lifeguard through Aquatic Management Group, was electrocuted at Heritage Point community pool on Labor Day weekend. She dipped her hand in the water to check chemicals. The grounding wire hadn't been inspected in 37 years. The Rosoff family became advocates for mandatory pool electrical inspections.

Cause of Death: Electrocution in flooded basement (died Sept 12 after removal from life support)
Facility Name: Private residence, 17200 block of Madison Road
Notes: Sandra Sharp, 54, was found unconscious in her flooded basement at 4:28 AM on Sept 10. CPR restored a pulse; she was hospitalized but taken off life support Sept 12. A box fan was found nearby but police called this 'speculative.' Not a dock/marina ESD — indoor flooded basement.

On October 7, 2016, a construction worker operating an industrial concrete saw contacted an energized electrical line in Shiloh, Illinois. He was rendered unconscious in approximately 6 inches of standing water. A rescuer was also shocked attempting to move the victim. Two Shiloh IL police officers used a canvas tie-down strap to extract the victim from the electrified water and performed CPR until paramedics arrived. The victim survived. The officers received a Life Saving Award from the Village Board of Trustees. Police Chief Jim Stover stated the victim would not have survived without the officers' training.

Cause of Death: Drowning (no electrical investigation conducted; ESD not ruled out)
Body of Water: River (specific river withheld in CDC report)
Facility Name: Marina on a river (name withheld by MIFACE to protect privacy)
Notes: In summer 2017, a male marina owner in his 80s drowned after falling into six-foot-deep water at a boat slip in his own marina on a river in Michigan. His spouse described him as a 'good swimmer' who had fallen in at least twice before without incident (when others were present to assist). On the day of his death he was working alone. He was found floating upright with the crown of his head visible above the water. Tools and keys were in the ignition of a nearby boat. The critical finding from the CDC FACE report (17MI208, published March 9, 2020) is that neither the sheriff nor the medical examiner investigator checked the water for stray electrical current. The combination of good swimmer, familiar marina environment, active shore power infrastructure, and absence of any ESD investigation makes this a textbook case of potential ESD misclassification.

Cause of Death: Drowning (fell into water while embarking/disembarking from boat)
Notes: On Friday, March 24, 2017, a 59-year-old male fell into the water at a marina dock in Monmouth Beach, New Jersey, while attempting to board or disembark from a boat. A 51-year-old female friend jumped in to rescue him. Both drowned. The rescuer-drowning pattern is a classic ESD indicator. However, a critical limiting factor is water salinity: Monmouth Beach borders the Shrewsbury River, a tidal inlet with brackish to saltwater composition, particularly in March. ESD is not possible in saltwater because saltwater conducts electricity around (not through) the body. The March date also raises hypothermia and cold shock as alternative explanations for rapid incapacitation.

Brothers Alex Lopez (12) and Isaiah Lopez (11) were electrocuted at Oakland Lake Park in Fort Worth. Alex stepped on a downed power line hidden in overgrown brush over 12 hours after morning storms. A friend told Isaiah, who went to check on his brother and was electrocuted when he touched Alex. Not a classic dock/marina ESD — downed power line incident.

Cause of Death: Electrocution — touched brother who was in contact with downed line

Two University of Alabama School of Law attorneys, Shelly Darling (34, Elder Law Clinic) and Elizabeth Whipple (41, Domestic Violence Clinic), died after going to sunbathe at a lakehouse dock on Lake Tuscaloosa. They went missing Friday evening; bodies recovered Saturday ~4:30 AM. A deputy was jolted by electricity on the dock during recovery. Autopsies confirmed electrocution. Lake Tuscaloosa had multiple ESD incidents (see also ESD-2015-08-08-1).

23-year-old man reportedly electrocuted when he extended an aluminum pole into the water around his sailboat at a private dock in Mandeville, LA. No independent sources could be located to verify this incident.

Facility Name: Private residence — basement with sump pump
Notes: On May 1, 2017, a homeowner in Rochester, New York rigged a sump pump and received a significant shock in his legs. A motor fault was the cause. No GFCI protection was in place. Non-marina context (residential basement).

Facility Name: De la Gappe Boulevard, Touraines neighbourhood — flooded street
Notes: On May 6, 2017, Richard Lafontaine was hospitalized in critical condition after suffering electric shock while building a sandbag barrier to protect homes on De la Gappe Boulevard in Gatineau, Quebec, during the catastrophic 2017 Ottawa River flooding. He fell into electrified floodwater on Saturday afternoon. Bystanders administered CPR and he was revived at the scene. As of May 12, he remained in hospital in serious condition. The water was electrified by electrical equipment in the flooded area. No GFCI protection was in place. His neighbor Diane Parisien, whom he was helping, participated in the rescue.

Carl McGrady, 65, was electrocuted entering his pool to clean it on May 19. His son Lucas, 35, jumped in to rescue him and was also electrocuted. Pam McGrady was shocked helping pull Carl out but survived. Carl died that evening; Lucas died June 2 after 14 days on life support at UAB Medical Center. Lucas was a father of three, engaged, and owned M&M Lawncare.

Cause of Death: Electrocution from downed power line in flooded field
Notes: Aldo Jordani Rojas, 14, was electrocuted after stepping into a storm-flooded puddle containing a downed power line in Laredo, TX. ESDPA incorrectly states two brothers were killed — only one victim died. The 'two brothers' detail likely comes from the separate Fort Worth/Lopez brothers incident (FI-2016-001/ESD-2017-03-29-1). Not a dock/marina ESD — downed power line incident.

Evan Currie, 19, a Xavier University sophomore from Dublin OH, was Ohio's first reported electric shock drowning victim. After docking at Miller Marina and plugging in shore power, the family dog fell in the water. Father Jeffrey entered and struggled; both sons jumped in. Evan's mother unplugged shore power, stopping the current. Evan was unresponsive, convulsing, and shouted 'electricity' between convulsions. Both boat and marina power were inspected and cleared.

Kayla Matos, 11, from Newark NJ, was visiting friends in Toms River when she was electrocuted in a lagoon. She and two friends were using an inflatable raft near a dormant boat lift with corroded electrical equipment. When the raft contacted the lift rail, Kayla touched the energized metal. All three girls were wearing life jackets. Adults were present and began CPR. She died at Community Medical Center. She was a 5th grader at Ann Street School and a Girl Scout.

Facility Name: Crab Corner, Menemsha Beach / transient dock
Notes: On June 21, 2017, several West Tisbury third graders on a school beach outing felt a 'charge' while swimming or wading at Crab Corner, Menemsha Beach, Chilmark, Martha's Vineyard, MA. Children experienced a 'small shock feeling' but were all fine. Harbormaster Dennis Jason confirmed the tingle when he reached his hand in the water. Power was cut at an electrical box on the bulkhead above the dock. The Town of Chilmark closed the swimming area. Follow-up investigation identified faulty grounding work from a contractor 5-8 years prior on the transient dock as the suspected source. Repairs were scheduled for July 12, 2017. The problem was described as 'recurrent.'

Five people died at a water park in Akyazi, Turkey: three children (12, 15, 17) were caught in electrical current in the pool; the 58-year-old park manager and his 30-year-old son jumped in to save them and were also killed. Two others were injured touching energized metal railings. The park lacked a residual current breaker. Included in ESDPA list because 'this could have happened in the USA.'

On July 3, 2017, a man's son grabbed the crossmember of a boat lift while under a boat at Lake Keowee, South Carolina. He was shocked but recovered. The boat lift was not properly grounded.
On July 7, 2017, two kayakers felt an electrical tingle at Canyon Lake, California. Authorities were investigating. The cause was unknown at the time of reporting.
On July 7, 2017, two kayakers felt an electrical tingle at Canyon Lake, California. Authorities were investigating. The cause was unknown at the time of reporting.

Cause of Death: Electrocution from frayed extension cord in bathtub
Notes: Madison Coe, 14, from Lubbock TX, was electrocuted while taking a bath at her father's home in Lovington, NM. She was using her phone (Samsung S6 Edge) plugged into a charger cord connected to a frayed extension cord in a non-GFCI outlet. She tried to keep connections dry but was unaware of the cord's frayed section. CPSC was involved in the investigation. Family released her final text/photo to raise awareness.

On July 23, 2017, Cameron Brooks (28) of Spallumcheen, BC attempted to rescue his two dogs from an electrified creek on his property. The dogs died immediately upon entering the water. Brooks waded in up to his thighs before the current overwhelmed him; he fell backwards and pulled himself out. He suffered leg burns and was airlifted to Kelowna General Hospital. Fire Chief Dave Cummings stated he would likely have died if he had gone deeper. The source was a faulty submersible irrigation pump. Wife April and two young children (Aemon, 2; Armoury, 6 months) were also at the property.

Cause of Death: Electrocution — sailboat mast struck overhead power line
Notes: Three Boy Scouts from Troop 620, Hallsville TX, were killed during a sailing lesson on Lake O' The Pines when their Hobie Cat catamaran's 30-foot mast struck a 7,200-volt overhead transmission line operated by Upshur Rural Electric Cooperative. The catamaran caught fire. Will Brannon (18) and Heath Faucheaux (16) died at the scene. Thomas Larry (11) was airlifted to Shreveport hospital and died August 7. All three were members of the Hallsville High School band. NESC requires 37.5 ft minimum clearance for lakes over 2,000 acres suitable for sailing; Lake O' The Pines is 18,680 acres. A nearly identical incident killed attorney G. Brockett Irwin at the same lake in December 1982.

Cole R. Grad, 15, a Chiawana High School student, was electrocuted while swimming at a private irrigation pump station on the Columbia River. He contacted an energized pipe connected to a faulty pump system. Two firefighters were also shocked during rescue. Coroner ruled death accidental from low voltage electrocution.

Facility Name: Greens Bayou near Interstate 10, east Houston
Notes: During Hurricane Harvey, volunteer rescuers and two Daily Mail journalists (7 total) were on a rescue boat that was pushed into downed power lines in Greens Bayou. Two confirmed dead (Yahir Vizueth, 25; Jorge Perez, 31), two missing/presumed dead (Benjamin Vizueth, 31; Gustavo Rodriguez-Hernandez, 40). Three survivors clung to trees for 18 hours. The group had successfully rescued 7 people before the tragedy.
